sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Cauda equina syndrome is a spinal emergency. Any physiotherapy setting that identifies suspicious symptoms must escalate urgently rather than trying to manage the presentation as routine back pain.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- New urinary retention or overflow incontinence
- Altered saddle sensation
- Bowel dysfunction
- Bilateral leg pain, weakness, or numbness
- Rapidly worsening neurological deficit with back pain

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Symptoms may be incomplete or evolving; absence of all classic features does not exclude the diagnosis
- Severe back pain with bilateral symptoms warrants caution even before bladder dysfunction is obvious
- Do not delay escalation for prolonged assessment, exercise, or routine treatment

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Ask directly about bladder, bowel, saddle symptoms, and major bilateral neurological change
- Stop routine treatment and arrange urgent medical escalation if concern exists
- Advise the patient not to drive themselves if significant neurological or bladder symptoms are present

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Document the exact symptoms, onset, and objective neurological findings
- Communicate clearly that this is a time-critical spinal emergency requiring urgent imaging and medical review
- Coordinate urgent transfer or emergency department referral according to setting and logistics

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - A low threshold for escalation is safer than waiting for the full syndrome to declare itself
